text: The video shows the left ovary with an adjacent eight-centimeter para-ovarian endometrioma visible on the left side.

The surgeon observes the left ovary with visible follicles and a large para-ovarian endometrioma adhered to the ovarian fossa. The right ovary is slightly adhered but lacks endometrioma. A puncture is made in the endometrioma using a harmonic vibrating jaw, followed by an enlargement of the opening to insert a suction device. The patient's head is elevated to prevent spilled cyst contents from reaching the upper abdomen, and the entire endometrial cyst is aspirated.

The cul-de-sac is visible with deposits of endometrial chocolate material, which are being suctioned out. The surgeon performs repeated suction and irrigation to clear the area. Adhesiolysis is necessary to separate the adhered endometrioma from the ovarian fossa.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to excise the para-ovarian cyst, applying andromedial traction with the left hand. The blunt tip of the suction irrigation device separates the ovarian cyst from the ovarian fossa, ensuring careful handling due to the proximity of the ureter. The excised cyst is successfully placed into an endo bag for removal.
The surgeon separates adhesions from the left utero-sacral ligament and the ovary adhered to the ovarian fossa. The cyst wall is further excised and completely removed.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation to clean the surgical area, removing residual chocolate material from the cyst. Following this, fulguration is applied to the bed of the cyst in the cul-de-sac to prevent recurrence.

The uterus is observed in an anti-verted position. The surgeon uses the suction irrigation tip to rub over the endometriotic area, removing debris. Subsequently, a spatula is used to cauterize the bed of the endometrial cyst, aiming to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon performs fulguration on the entire ovarian side of the para-ovarian cyst to eliminate any remaining endometriotic material and prevent recurrence. The ovarian fossa, where the ovary was previously adhered, is also fulgurated to burn any residual endometriotic tissue.

The surgeon performs fulguration using electrosurgery, maintaining a 0.5 millimeter distance from the tissue to create a spray effect. This technique minimizes depth of injury while maximizing coverage, resembling a paint spray over the tissue surface.

The surgeon uses a spatula to perform fulguration on areas affected by endometriosis, including the posterior wall of the uterus, cul-de-sac, and uterosacral ligament. Following fulguration, repeated suction and irrigation are conducted to remove burnt tissue.

The surgeon performs final irrigation and suction to remove remaining blood and fulgurated tissue char, revealing a clean surgical field with no bleeding or oozing. The fluid appears clear, indicating successful removal of debris. An interseed is recommended to prevent adhesion formation.

The surgeon maintains the uterus in an antiverted position and places a 15x15 interseed into the cul-de-sac, posterior wall of the uterus, and ovarian fossa to prevent adhesion and potentially reduce the recurrence of endometriosis. The tubal patency test confirms that both fallopian tubes are patent.

The surgeon spreads a sheet of reoxygenated cellulose (Interseed) between the ovary and ovarian fossa to prevent adhesion. The material, resembling tissue paper, is carefully placed to cover the posterior wall of the uterus and the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring it does not cover the ovary itself.

The surgeon uses two-hand instruments, including a grasper and a Maryland, to carefully spread the interseed over the uterosacral ligament, ovary, and ovarian fossa. The ovary is lifted to position the interseed posteriorly, ensuring it covers the left side ovarian fossa where fulguration was performed. The interseed appears shredded in the area.
The surgeon ensures the interseed is spread over the surgical area, while the assistant gradually releases the uterine manipulation, allowing the uterus to drop into its natural position.
The surgeon ensures the interseed is properly adhered around the surgical site, confirming the area is dry with no bleeding or oozing. The gloves endo bag is prepared for tissue retrieval.

The surgeon places the removed cyst into an endo bag using a ten millimeter umbilical port. The endo bag is then extracted through the port using a semi-traumatic clasp, with both edges of the glove sleeve pulled to facilitate removal. Once outside, the bag is opened and the contents are pulled out using an Oham forceps.
The surgeon prepares to close the dilated port using a varus needle after the removal of the endo bag.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to pierce one side of the wound, excluding the skin, and inserts a loop suture. A grasper pulls the real suture into the loop suture, which then extracts the real suture. The loop suture functions similarly to a cobbler's needle, pulling the suture out for knot tying outside the wound to prevent hernia at the ten millimeter port site.
This was just a simple case of endometrioma. Have a nice day. God bless you.
